notes: Approximately 75% of data mining work is getting data to point where algorithms can run
Supplier's vertical industry focus is key selection criterion; domain knowledge reduces costs
Data mining toolset should run on customer's existing server platform
Breadth=multiple DM functions; depth=multiple algorithms per function; depth gives flexibility
Five qualifiers: lifecycle support, architecture, ease of use, professional services, company strength
Pricing of toolset is fifth criterion; price/performance comparison enables ranking
Four industries analyzed: financial services, retail, telecommunications, other verticals
Decision trees help perform prediction function
Neural networks help perform prediction function
Association rules help perform affinity function
Parallelizing software harnesses multiple CPUs to scale up and speedup compute-intensive analysis against huge databases
Thinking Machines filed Chapter 11 in August 1994 despite being parallel computing pioneer
Line of business should lead DM project with cross-functional team; not IT-only initiative
Buying guide specifically evaluates scalable commercial-grade tools; distinguished from research tools
ANGOSS had strength across financial services, retail, and telecom verticals
NeoVista focused on retail, financial services, and insurance enterprises
Data mining offers technology to systematize excavation of value from databases
Value from data mining: programs to stimulate customers to buy more, buy first time, stay loyal
Significant differences in tool types, expertise levels, financial health require careful vendor evaluation
Thinking Machines already in Chapter 11 in 1994 before this guide; guide includes despite bankruptcy status
HyperParallel acquired by Yahoo in 1996 — one of Yahoo's early strategic data mining acquisitions
NeoVista acquired by Accrue Software 1999; NeoVista products purchased by JDA Software Group in 2001
Data Distilleries acquired by SPSS in 2000; SPSS acquired by IBM for $1.2B in 2009
ANGOSS survived; acquired by Peterson Partners 2013; Datawatch acquired for $24.5M 2018; now Altair product
Data mining evolved into ML/AI; became dominant enterprise and consumer technology worth $300B+ by 2026
Neural networks became deep learning; transformers and LLMs (GPT-4, etc.) became dominant AI paradigm 2017-2023+
2020s ML practitioners confirm 75-80% of ML work remains data preparation/feature engineering
Data mining is a business issue; it should improve the bottom line; LOB should lead
Data mining suppliers differ significantly in tool types, expertise levels, financial health
By misapplying commercial-grade data mining tool an enterprise can squander potential and possibly millions of dollars
